statement.p: An airplane was cruising at {34000\ {\rm ft}}\text{,} and then began its descent. Because of some air traffic congestion, it will descend to {9400\ {\rm ft}} and then circle the airport for a while. It descends at a rate of {910\ {\textstyle\frac{\rm\mathstrut ft}{\rm\mathstrut min}}}\text{.} A passenger notices the plane is still descending. Set up an inequality where the solution set represents how much time might have passed since the plane started its descent. There is no need to solve it.

solution.p: This is a rate exercise, and we can use

The question mark in the above is not an equal sign, because we are trying to set up an inequality. The initial value for the altitude is 34000\text{,} the altitude is decreasing (so we will subtract, not add), the rate is 910\text{,} and the final altitude is 9400\text{.} We do not know is how much time has passed, so we decide to use t to represent that time. At the moment the passenger makes their observation, the plane is still descending, so the altitude is greater than the final value. So our inequality is:
